planting.note: Plant container-grown shrubs or rooted cuttings in spring; flame acanthus establishes quickly in well-drained soil. In zone 7–9 it dies to the ground in winter and returns vigorously from the root each spring. Full sun and dry conditions produce the most compact, floriferous plants.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained, poor to moderately fertile soil; flame acanthus is a Texas native that thrives in the limestone soils and summer heat of zone 8–9 with minimal supplemental irrigation once established. The tubular orange-red flowers, produced on arching branches from summer through fall frost, are among the most effective hummingbird attractants in the western United States — plants in bloom are visited constantly by Ruby-throated and Black-chinned hummingbirds. Cut back to 6–12 inches in late winter to encourage dense new growth; avoid fertilizing, as rich soil produces lush foliage at the expense of flowers. This native shrub is excellent in butterfly gardens, xeriscape designs, and naturalistic borders.
sources: Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center — Anisacanthus quadrifidus var. wrightii
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Texas Native Shrubs
Texas Parks and Wildlife — Native Plants for Texas Gardens

planting.note: Start seeds or cuttings indoors 8 weeks before last frost; plant out after all frost risk passes. In zone 9b–10 crossandra grows as a perennial ground cover or border plant, blooming nearly year-round; in cooler parts of zone 9 it performs as a summer annual or container plant brought indoors for winter.

directions: Plant in partial shade to full sun in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic soil; crossandra is one of the few brightly-colored flowering plants that blooms reliably in partial shade, making it valuable for brightening shaded beds in the South. The salmon-orange to red flowers are borne in dense, upright spikes above glossy, dark-green foliage from spring through fall in warm climates. The plant thrives in humid heat and is widely used in Indian gardens and as a temple flower (kanakambaram) in southern India. Maintain consistent moisture and fertilize monthly with a balanced fertilizer during the growing season; plants become straggly after 2–3 years and should be replaced with rooted cuttings.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Crossandra

planting.note: Plant bulbs 2–3 inches deep in well-drained soil in spring; rain lilies naturalize freely and spread by offsets in zone 7–10. They bloom reliably after heavy summer rains, hence the common name. Leave clumps undivided for maximum impact — the denser the colony, the more spectacular the post-rain bloom display.

directions: Plant small bulbs 2–3 inches deep in full sun in well-drained soil; rain lilies are among the easiest and most rewarding of all small bulbs for Southern gardens, requiring virtually no care once established. The magic of rain lilies is their trigger — after a significant summer rain, the entire colony bursts into bloom within 24–48 hours, producing a carpet of pink, funnel-shaped flowers that last 2–3 days before fading. This cycle repeats with each substantial rain through summer and early fall. Naturalize under trees and in lawn edges where they can be left undisturbed; the fine, grass-like foliage is nearly invisible between blooming events. Divide crowded clumps every 3–4 years to maintain vigor.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Zephyranthes in Florida
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Rain Lilies for Texas Gardens
Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center — Zephyranthes grandiflora

planting.note: Plant small corms 2–3 inches deep in fall; in zone 9 cape sorrel grows through winter and spring, blooming in March–April, then going dormant in summer heat. It naturalized across the Gulf Coast as an edible winter weed. Harvest leaves and flowers from December through April before dormancy.

directions: Plant small bulbils in fall in partial shade to full sun in well-drained, sandy soil; cape sorrel is a Mediterranean import that has naturalized across California, the Gulf Coast, and many mild-winter regions as a winter-growing edible. The clover-like leaves and bright yellow flowers are both edible — the leaves have a pleasantly tart, lemony flavor from oxalic acid and can be used in salads, stir-fries, or as a garnish in the same way as garden sorrel. The bright yellow flowers are also edible and attractive as a garnish. Note that like all oxalis, cape sorrel should be consumed in moderation as oxalic acid can interfere with calcium absorption in large quantities. Plants die down completely in summer heat and re-emerge from dormant corms in fall.
sources: University of California Cooperative Extension — Oxalis pes-caprae
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Edible Wild Plants
USDA NRCS Plants Database — Oxalis pes-caprae

planting.note: Start indoors 4 weeks before last frost or direct sow after last frost once soil is warm; in zone 9 angled luffa can be planted March through May for a summer harvest. Unlike smooth luffa, angled luffa is harvested primarily as a tender vegetable at 6–10 inches long, not allowed to mature into a sponge.

directions: Grow on a sturdy trellis or arbor in full sun in fertile, well-drained soil; angled luffa (turiya in Gujarati, turai in Hindi) is a staple vegetable in South Asian cuisine, prized for its delicate, slightly bitter flavor and ability to hold up in cooked dishes. Unlike the smooth luffa used for sponges, the angled luffa has prominent longitudinal ridges and must be harvested young at 6–10 inches while the flesh is tender and the ridges peel off easily; older fruits become fibrous and bitter. The plant grows vigorously in summer heat and humidity, producing heavily if given consistent moisture and regular fertilization. Harvest every 2–3 days to prevent fruits from maturing on the vine.
sources: Asian Vegetable Research and Development Center — Ridge Gourd
Cornell University Small Farms Program — Asian Gourds
